Announcement from Frode, Warden of the Astral Ward
Another Awakened informant has reached a raven post to report village dissenters.

Another exploration team returned earlier. I've pored over their findings, and one thing that's becoming increasingly clear is that this just couldn't have been a major population center. There are no homes, few living areas at all, and none of the infrastructure needed for a town, much less a city.    I'll concede that some of these structures are massive, and obviously this place served some very important purpose. But this is not the lost capital city of the seers that others seem to think it is. Determining why they chose Castora and what they built here will be an important focal point going forward, but we need to be careful to see only what is actually there.    Some of my more optimistic colleagues think that the capital city must be further inland, and I can understand why. This civilization was once the mightiest in Tyria, so surely we must have simply not yet found the ruins they left behind.    But I offer instead a sobering theory: we will never find it, because it's gone. The mursaat cast down every stone they found, and the only reason the ruins remain here is because they never found this island. We should treasure everything we uncover here, because it's likely to be the last we ever find.

Another retired scientist came by the lab today, bringing warnings of the dangers to come if we proceed with the project. It's true that we haven't completely solved the alignment problems, but progress can't be made if we stop and wait to fix every potential issue in advance. When we encounter mistakes we will correct them; same as always.Still, some of our investors have expressed their reservations. No matter. The project will go ahead as planned. In a decade, when our jade mechs have brought this city into a golden age, they'll thank us for not pausing to wait for them to regain their stomachs.
Another team from your guild is in a match. Please wait.

Another wave of them enter and the screams continue; horrible, pitiful screams echoing off of every wall. And yet more of those freebooters go in, chasing the promise of treasure that Kuda dangles in front of them. What greed could power them through it? The lucky ones are killed instantly, impaled on spikes or atomized by ancient magic. The unlucky die slowly over hours, wailing in the dark, begging for rescue that will never come, praying for death, while it toys with them like a cat. We do our best to ignore the sounds, knowing that if the freebooters fail, it will undoubtedly be us crying helplessly into the uncaring void.    Alchemy, what am I doing here? I just wanted to be able to pursue my research without the council and the colleges impeding me at every step. And even now, in the mouth of the abyss, I hate them still. In my first year with the Inquest, I accomplished more than a decade working within their system. So yes, I hate them all the more for not leaving me any choice, for making the Inquest the better option.    I cling to one foolish hope: the director doesn't approve of Kuda's methods, and her krewe speak of it often. Maybe he doesn't know the conditions we're being subjected to. Perhaps if I can find some way to inform him, something can still be done. But, more likely, my time will soon be up.

Any analysis of the Searing would be incomplete without exploring the history of magical escalation in conflicts throughout Tyria. Attempting to determine which party was justified in using overwhelming force does not help us predict future catastrophes, but a pattern emerges when we examine who had reason to believe that they were cornered.    This belief does not need to be accurate to lead to tragedy, and taking the final step is rarely a sound tactical decision. Once one party has used devastating arcane weapons to claim a decisive victory, the long-term results are more often a chain of reckless last stands and societal collapse.
